elements.sent-2411-001: In data-based control, dissipativity can be a powerful tool for attaining stability guarantees for nonlinear systems if that dissipativity can be inferred from data.
elements.sent-2411-002: This work provides a tutorial on several existing methods for data-based dissipativity estimation of nonlinear systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: The interplay between the underlying assumptions of these methods and their sample complexity is investigated.
elements.sent-2411-004: It is shown that methods based on $\delta$-covering result in an intractable trade-off between sample complexity and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-005: A new method is proposed to quantify the robustness of machine learning-based dissipativity estimation.
elements.sent-2411-006: It is shown that this method achieves a more tractable trade-off between robustness and sample complexity.
elements.sent-2411-007: Several numerical case studies demonstrate the results.
elements.sent-2411-008: There has been significant interest recently in data-based control, where a control policy is either designed directly from input-output data, or a plant model is identified from data to inform controller design \cite{Zhao2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Many of these methods rely on linearity of the unknown system to attain stability guarantees with relatively little data.
elements.sent-2411-010: For nonlinear systems, attaining such guarantees is more challenging \cite{Martin2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In model-based control, identifying a dissipative characterization of the nonlinear system is one approach to assuring stability.
elements.sent-2411-012: Dissipativity is a input-output property that generalizes gain, passivity, and conic sectors, among others.
elements.sent-2411-013: Given a dissipative plant, the Dissipativity Theorem \cite{Vidyasagar1981 provides tractable constraints on the open-loop properties of a controller that guarantee closed-loop stability.}
elements.sent-2411-014: This constraint can used to recover robust stability in optimal control problems based on a nominal linearization \cite{Geromel1997a .}
elements.sent-2411-015: There are many model-based tools for characterizing dissipativity \cite{Hill1980 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Characterizing dissipativity directly from data would enable robust design strategies for data-based control.
elements.sent-2411-017: If the dissipativity characterization identified from a finite data set is guaranteed to hold for all possible system trajectories, the it is said to be ``robust".
elements.sent-2411-018: This robustness is critical for guaranteeing stability through the Dissipativity Theorem.
elements.sent-2411-019: Robust dissipativity estimation methods for linear systems based on Willems' Fundamental Lemma \cite{Willems2005 are well-developed and have data requirements as low as a single trajectory from a persistently exciting input \cite{Koch2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: For nonlinear systems, achieving robustness is more difficult and requires much more data.
elements.sent-2411-021: Some approaches have relaxed the problem to allow for a partially known model or full state access \cite{Martin2021,Martin2023TAC .}
elements.sent-2411-022: This work reviews methods for off-line robust dissipativity estimation of unknown nonlinear systems with only input-output data \cite{Montenbruck2016,Romer2017,RomerGaussian,Tang2019,Tang2019journal,Tang2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The contribution of this work is primarily tutorial.
elements.sent-2411-024: Section~\ref{sec:l2 clarifies the assumptions that underlie existing methods for robust nonlinear dissipativity estimation and investigates the consequences of those assumptions on the claimed robustness.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Section~\ref{sec:delta then reviews a class of ``$\delta$-covering" methods \cite{Montenbruck2016,Romer2017,RomerGaussian and provides a modest extension thereof.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: It is demonstrated that for these methods, either the sample complexity is too high to be implemented, or the desired robustness property does not hold as expected.
elements.sent-2411-027: Section~\ref{sec:ML reviews a class of methods based on machine learning techniques \cite{Tang2019,Tang2019journal,Tang2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: A new approach to verify robustness for these methods is proposed based on the generalization error from probably approximately correct learning \cite{ShalevShwartz2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: It is shown that this generalization error decouples the sample complexity from the assumptions investigated in Section~\ref{sec:l2 , so robustness can be practically achieved given an appropriate data generation method.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Several such methods are explored in numerical examples.
elements.sent-2411-031: The set of strictly positive real numbers is denoted $\bb{R _>$, and $\bb{R _\geq \defeq \bb{R _>\cup \{0\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The set of $n$-dimensional real vectors is denoted $\bb{R ^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The real and imaginary components of a complex number, $x$, are $\mathrm{Re (x)$ and $\mathrm{Im (x)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The identity matrix is $I$, and $(\cdot)^T$ and $(\cdot)^{-1}$ denote the transpose and inverse of real matrices.
elements.sent-2411-035: If $x\in\bb{R}$, then $|x|$ denotes the absolute value.
elements.sent-2411-036: If $\mathcal{S}$ is a set, then $|\mathcal{S |$ is its cardonality.}
elements.sent-2411-037: A function $f:\bb{R ^n\rightarrow\bb{R}$ has complexity $\mathcal{O (g(x))$, denoted $f(x)\sim\mathcal{O (g(x))$, if there exists $k,x_0\in\bb{R _>$ and $g:\bb{R ^n\rightarrow\bb{R}$ such that $|f(x)|\leq kg(x)$ for all $x\geq x_0$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Conversely, $f(x)\sim\Omega(g(x))$ if $|f(x)| \geq kg(x)$ for all $x\geq x_0$.
elements.sent-2411-039: This provides a lower bound on complexity.
elements.sent-2411-040: Time, space, and sample complexity refer to the amount of computations, storage, and samples required to execute an algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-041: Let $\mathcal{X}$ be a real inner product space with inner product $\langle(\cdot),(\cdot)\rangle:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\bb{R}$ and induced norm $\|x\|\defeq\sqrt{\langle x,x\rangle <\infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: If the elements of $\mathcal{X}$ are $n$-dimensional vector sequences, i.e. $x:\bb{R}\rightarrow\bb{R ^n$ for all $x\in\mathcal{X}$, then the space is denoted $\mathcal{X ^n$, when relevant.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: The extension of $\mathcal{X}$, denoted $\mathcal{X _e$, satisfies $\|x\|_T^2 \defeq \|x_{T}\|^2<\infty$ for all $T\in \bb{R _\geq$, where $x_{T}$ is the truncation of $x(k)$ at $k=T$, defined as $x(k) = x(k)$ for $k\leq T$ and $x(k) = 0$ for $k>T$.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The truncated inner product is $\langle x,y\rangle_T \defeq \langle x_T,y_T\rangle$ for all $x,y\in\mathcal{X}$.
elements.sent-2411-045: The space of square integrable functions is $\mathcal{L _2$, which has inner product $\int_0^\infty x^Ty dt$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The Frobenius norm and $\mathcal{L _\infty$ norm are denoted $\|(\cdot)\|_F$ and $\|\cdot\|_{\mathcal{L _\infty}$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: When not specified, the induced norm of the relevant Hilbert space is assumed.
elements.sent-2411-048: Let $\widehat{u}$ denote the Fourier transform of $u:\mathbb{R}\rightarrow\mathbb{R ^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: If $\mathcal{G}$ is an LTI system, then $\widehat{\mathcal{G (\omega)$ denotes its transfer function.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: An operator $\mathcal{G :\mathcal{U _e\rightarrow \mathcal{Y _e$ is $(Q,S,R)$-dissipative if \begin{equation}\langle y,Qy\rangle_T + \langle y,Su \rangle_T + \langle u,Ru\rangle_T \geq 0 \label{eqn:diss_orig}\end{equation} for all $u\in\mathcal{U _e$ and $T\in\bb{R _>$, where $Q$, $S$, and $R$ are real matrices of appropriate dimensions.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: An operator is ultimately virtual $(Q,S,R)$-dissipative if \begin{equation}\langle y,Qy\rangle + \langle y,Su \rangle + \langle u,Ru\rangle \geq 0 \label{eqn:diss}\end{equation} for all $u\in\mathbf{K (\mathcal{G ) \defeq \{u\in\mathcal{U \, | \, y\in\mathcal{Y}\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: If, in addition, $\mathbf{K (\mathcal{G ) = \mathcal{U}$, then $\mathcal{G}$ is ultimately $(Q,S,R)$-dissipative.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: An interior conic sector is $\mathrm{cone _c(r)\defeq (-I,(r^2{- c^2I,2cI)$-dissipative or $\mathrm{cone (a,b)\defeq $ $(-I,\frac{a+b {2 I,-abI)$-dissipative, where $c$, $r$, $a$, and $b$ are the center, radius, lower bound, and upper bound, respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Degenerate conic bounds are $\mathrm{cone (a,\infty) \defeq (0,\frac{1 {2 I,-aI)$ and $\mathrm{cone (-\infty,b) \defeq (0,-\frac{1 {2 I,b)$ \cite{Zames1966 .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: For Definition~\ref{def:diss to have practical utility for robust control, the space $\mathcal{U _e$ must be rich enough to represent all possible inputs that the system will encounter during operation.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: This is usually taken to be the space $\mathcal{L _{2e}$, which is the space of all signals with finite energy over a finite time domain.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Verifying \autoref{eqn:diss_orig for each signal in $\mathcal{L _{2e}$ independently would require infinite data.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: This problem is easily circumvented for LTI systems because their behavior can be fully characterized by their response to either a persistently exciting input (via Willems' Fundamental Theorem \cite{Willems2005 ) or the set $\mathcal{U _e = \{\sin(\omega t) \; \forall \; \omega\in\bb{R _\geq\}$ (as in the classical approach to experimental Nyquist analysis \cite{Nyquist1932 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: These simplifications do not hold for nonlinear systems.
elements.sent-2411-060: Therefore, existing methods \cite{Montenbruck2016,Romer2017,RomerGaussian,Tang2021 make several assumptions on $\mathcal{U _e$.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The first two have been explicitly stated in various ways in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-062: The second is necessary because arbitrarily small input signals cannot be densely sampled.
elements.sent-2411-063: It is also practical for maintaining a sufficient signal-to-noise ratio in data collection.
elements.sent-2411-064: The third assumption below has not been explicitly stated in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-065: If $\mathcal{G}$ is causal and $Q$ is negative definite (which encompasses many, but not all, important cases), then ultimate virtual dissipativity implies dissipativity \cite[Theorem 1]{Hill1980 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Therefore, Assumption 3 is often justified as an approximation for dissipativity.
elements.sent-2411-067: Applying these three assumptions to $\mathcal{L _{2e}$ results in an input space that can be represented with an infinite set of orthonormal basis functions, such as Legendre polynomials, Fourier bases, or wavelets.}
elements.sent-2411-068: To make the problem tractable, one last assumption is usually made.
elements.sent-2411-069: Therefore, if the neglected basis functions encode high-frequency information, their impact on the operator's dissipativity is expected to be negligible.
elements.sent-2411-072: Consequently, the dissipativity properties of a system on $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ tend to the system's properties on $\mathcal{L _{2e}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Nonetheless, to verify dissipativity on $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ there remain infinitely many $u\in\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ to test.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The following sections review the major existing strategies to guarantee dissipativity on $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ using a finite sample set.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Section~\ref{sec:delta shows that the sample complexity of $\delta$-covering methods is so large that a system's behavior on $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ is unlikely to represent its behavior on $\mathcal{L _{2e}$ with a practical sample size.}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Then, Section~\ref{sec:ML shows that machine learning methods can derive probabilistic guarantees of dissipativity on $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ without this sample complexity problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: In \cite{Montenbruck2016 , an $\mathcal{L _2$-norm $\delta$-covering of $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ is proposed as a way of constructing guaranteed dissipativity properties in the special cases of gain and passivity indices, which was also used for general SISO dissipativity in \cite{Romer2017 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: This method requires an additional assumption on the unknown system.

elements.sent-2411-081: The resulting dissipativity characterization is conservative, and the exact characterization is achieved at $\delta \rightarrow 0$.

elements.sent-2411-083: Further, $K \sim \Omega(\rho^b)$ for any sampling procedure because it takes $\rho^b$ samples to $\delta$-cover $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ in the $\mathcal{L _\infty$ norm, and $\mathcal{B _\delta^{\mathcal{L _2}\subseteq \mathcal{B _\delta^{\mathcal{L _\infty}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Therefore, while more efficient methods than \autoref{eqn:sampling could be derived for generating a $\delta$-cover, all will have super-exponential sample complexity with respect to the number of bases.}

elements.sent-2411-090: One consequence of the extreme sample complexity is that $\delta$-covering methods are restricted to systems low-dimensional inputs, as noted in \cite{Tang2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-091: What has been less noted is that even for single-input systems, $b$ must be small because $K\sim \mathcal{O (b^b)$.}
elements.sent-2411-092: This is noticeable in the literature, where all existing case studies consider $b\leq 5$ \cite{Montenbruck2016,Romer2017,RomerGaussian,Tang2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-093: This section demonstrates that for small $b$, the dissipativity properties guaranteed on $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ can be vastly different than the dissipativity properties that hold on $\mathcal{L _{2e}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-098: Therefore, the extremum gain responses for the data set are achieved by sampling each basis independently.

elements.sent-2411-127: A variation on the $\delta$-covering method uses a Gaussian process to represent uncertainty in the dissipativity (specifically, passivity index), and applies Bayesian optimization to sequentially sample regions of $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ with the largest uncertainty \cite{RomerGaussian .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: This has the potential to significantly reduce the number of samples necessary to achieve the same accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-129: However, it is noted in \cite{RomerGaussian that this method will similarly struggle from the curse of dimensionality with large $b$, and the computational cost per sample is larger than the $\delta$-covering methods.}
elements.sent-2411-130: More work is needed to determine if the Gaussian process method can be extended to more general dissipativity and if it results in sub-exponential sample complexity relative to $b$.
elements.sent-2411-131: An alternative to the $\delta$-covering is to represent dissipativity estimation as a machine learning problem \cite{Tang2019journal,Tang2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-132: Instead of bounding the worst-case trajectories with a $\delta$-cover or Gaussian process, machine learning methods aim to achieve statistical guarantees that the the data is sufficiently descriptive.
elements.sent-2411-133: A variety of machine learning methods have been proposed \cite{Tang2021 ; however, in most cases, error bounds and complexity analysis have not been discussed, or have been discussed in terms of unverifiable assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Here, we attempt to close that gap, focusing on the one-class support vector machine (OC-SVM) method from \cite{Tang2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-135: The hard (not allowing for any categorization error) OC-SVM solves \begin{equation}\min_{\Pi,\rho} \|\Pi\|_F^2/2 - \rho s.t. \langle \Pi,\Gamma(u_i)\rangle\geq \rho \forall \; u_i\inS, \label{eqn:OC-SVM}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{S}$ is the set of sample trajectories, $\rho{\in}\mathbb{R _+$ measures the distance of the samples from the half-plane, and $\|\Pi\|_F^2/2$ penalizes the boundary complexity \cite{Tang2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-136: Equation~\ref{eqn:OC-SVM seeks the half-plane closest to the data for which all data appears on only one side.}
elements.sent-2411-137: This gives the tightest $(Q,S,R)$ characterization of the data.
elements.sent-2411-138: The question remains whether $\mathcal{S}$ is adequately informative.
elements.sent-2411-139: In probably approximately correct (PAC) learning \cite[\S 3]{ShalevShwartz2014 , the following assumption is used.}
elements.sent-2411-140: This is written $L_\mathcal{D (\Pi) \defeq \underset{u_i\sim\mathcal{D {\mathbb{P }\left(\langle \Pi,\Gamma(u_i)\rangle < 0\right).$ On the other hand, the empirical loss is $L_\mathcal{S (\Pi) \defeq |\{\mathcal{S}\, | \, \langle \Pi,\Gamma(u_i)\rangle < 0\}|/|\mathcal{S |.$ For noiseless samples, $\{u_i,\mathcal{G (u_i)\}$, of a $(Q,S,R)$-dissipative system, $\Pi(Q,S,R)$ results in $L_\mathcal{S (\Pi) = 0$ by definition, though it may be appropriate to allow for nonzero loss for noisy data.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: More importantly, the generalization error, $|L_\mathcal{D -L_\mathcal{S |$, measures how well $\Pi$ generalizes from $\mathcal{S}$ to the rest of $\mathcal{U _e$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Assuming $L_\mathcal{S = 0$, a bound with probability $1-\delta$ on the generalization error of OC-SVM is given in \cite{Scholkopf2001 as \begin{equation}\underset{u_i\simD}{P} \left(\langle \Pi,\Gamma(u_i)\rangle < \rho - \gamma \right) \leq \frac{2}{K}\left(\log_2\left(\frac{K^2}{2\delta}\right)+\frac{16c^2}{\hat{\gamma}^2}\log_2\left(\frac{\ln(2)}{4c^2}\hat{\gamma}^2K\right) +2\right),\end{equation} where $K$ is the number of samples, $c = 103$, $\hat{\gamma = \gamma/\|\Pi\|_F$, and $\gamma\in\mathbb{R _+$ is a parameter that relaxes the offset distance $\rho$ to allow for data points closer to the boundary of the half-plane.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: Since only $\langle \Pi,\Gamma(u_i)\rangle \geq 0$ is required, $\gamma=\rho$ should be chosen to minimize the number of samples necessary.
elements.sent-2411-144: It's noted in \cite{Scholkopf2001 that $c$ may be 50 times smaller in practice, and tighter bounds may have become available since the original work.}
elements.sent-2411-145: In any case, this provides a calculable probabilistic guarantee that future samples will be accurately characterized.
elements.sent-2411-146: Importantly, this bound does not scale with the number of basis functions of $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ or with the input and output dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-147: This generalization error is only achieved if the data is sampled according to distribution $\mathcal{D}$, the meaning of which is not immediately clear.
elements.sent-2411-148: One interpretation is to sample uniformly from $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$, which supposes that every such signal is equally likely to occur.}
elements.sent-2411-149: This is essentially the perspective taken in \cite{Tang2021 , where input trajectories are generated by uniformly sampling the bounded coefficients ($\alpha_i$ in Assumption 4) of $b$ Fourier bases.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Since a $\delta$-covering is not needed to achieve confidence in the results, and since the generalization bound does not grow with the data dimension, a large number of basis functions can be used to represent $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$, making it a better approximation of $\mathcal{L _{2e}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: This is demonstrated in Figure~\ref{fig:ML , which depicts the upper and lower bounds of 1000 trajectories of \autoref{eqn:example randomly sampled from $\mathcal{U _{A1234}$ with different numbers of basis functions over $T=20$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-152: As with the $\delta$-covering method, a long time horizon results in a good characterization of the upper conic bound (about 4\% error in every case), and more basis functions result in a better characterizations of the lower conic bound (almost 300\% error with $b=2$, and only 14.7\% error with $b=100$).

elements.sent-2411-155: A second interpretation is to randomly generate input signals from a Weiner process.
elements.sent-2411-156: This is the perspective implicitly taken in \cite{Tang2019,Tang2019journal .}
elements.sent-2411-157: A Weiner process is the limit of a random walk, so at each time step, the Weiner process samples randomly from all possible next time steps.
elements.sent-2411-158: This approach does not require Assumptions 1, 2, or 4.
elements.sent-2411-159: However, the Weiner process has a tendency to move away from its origin over time, which amplifies the contribution of low-frequency information.
elements.sent-2411-160: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:dithering , the system's response converges to its zero-frequency response as $T\rightarrow\infty$, failing to characterize the lower conic bound for \autoref{eqn:example .}}
elements.sent-2411-161: This trend is in conflict with the justification of Assumption 3, which suggests that $T$ should be chosen as large as possible to approximate ultimate virtual dissipativity.
elements.sent-2411-162: One solution to this is to use Weiner processes with uniformly sampled time lengths, $T\in[T_\mathrm{min ,T_\mathrm{max ]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-163: Or, more efficiently, calculate the dissipativity in response to a single or a few Weiner processes for all $T=\Delta t,2\Delta t,\dots,T_\mathrm{max}$, with some increment $\Delta t$.
elements.sent-2411-164: Either way, Assumption 3 can be removed, and dissipativity can be estimated directly instead of inferred from ultimate virtual dissipativity.
elements.sent-2411-165: This is depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:dithering2 , which shows short Weiner process inputs invoke high-frequency response, while long Weiner process inputs invoke low-frequency response.}
elements.sent-2411-166: For large enough $T_\mathrm{max}$ and small enough $T_\mathrm{min}$, the cone is well characterized by the data.
elements.sent-2411-167: Choosing $u_i$ to be Weiner processes with different lengths appears to work exceptionally well for the simple examples studied here.
elements.sent-2411-168: However, this result should be interpreted cautiously when extending to general nonlinear systems.
elements.sent-2411-169: A Weiner process results in an input signal that is persistently exciting, so this is effectively a circuitous application of Willems' Fundamental Lemma when applied to linear systems \cite{Willems2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-170: Nonetheless, the interpretation of a Weiner process sampling from the underlying distribution, $\mathcal{D}$, of signals in $\mathcal{L _{2e}$ is an appealing extension to nonlinear systems.}
